title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder

tex_statement.html: A CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_2 -- "AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_2 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_2}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_2}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Lem}[AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder]
        A CDMG  $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% Proof lifted verbatim from lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex, lines 228-244
% (the \Claude{...} block immediately following the Lem); the outer \Claude{%
% ... }%end Claude wrapper is stripped.
\begin{proof}
($\Leftarrow$)
Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$.
If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$),
then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

\medskip\noindent($\Rightarrow$)
Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph.
Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$
and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$.
Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$
(otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle).
Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$.
If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$---unless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Lemma — AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder

A CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.

Proof.

($\Leftarrow$) Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$. If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$), then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph. Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$ and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$. Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$ (otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle). Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$. If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$—unless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.

lean_code_with_comments:
/-- claim_3_2 (`AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder`): a CDMG `G` is acyclic
iff it has a topological order. Mirrors
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` Lem at line 224 verbatim,
using `CDMG.IsAcyclic` (def_3_6) on the left and the existential
`CDMG.HasTopologicalOrder` (def_3_8) on the right. The `[Finite α]`
hypothesis is needed for the (⇒) direction's parent-free-node
extraction; the (⇐) direction does not use finiteness. The proof
phase can recover a concrete `Fintype α` instance from `Finite α`
via `Fintype.ofFinite` if the constructive enumeration is needed. -/
theorem isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder
    [Finite α] (G : CDMG α) :
    G.IsAcyclic ↔ G.HasTopologicalOrder := by
  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
  · -- (⇒) acyclic ⇒ has topological order.
    -- The LN constructs the order by iteratively picking a parent-
    -- free node from the induced subgraph on the still-unselected
    -- nodes (requires finiteness). We use a cleaner Mathlib path:
    -- the relation `r₀ v w := ∃ directed walk v → w` is a partial
    -- order under `IsAcyclic` (reflexive by the trivial walk,
    -- transitive by walk concatenation, antisymmetric because any
    -- two-way directed walk would compose to a directed cycle),
    -- and `extend_partialOrder` (Szpilrajn extension) gives a
    -- linear order `s ≥ r₀`. The strict part `lt v w := s v w ∧
    -- v ≠ w` is then a topological order of `G`. The `[Finite α]`
    -- hypothesis is unused on this path; we keep it for
    -- compatibility with the LN's stated form and for downstream
    -- callers who naturally carry it.
    intro hac
    -- The "reachable by a directed walk" preorder.
    let r₀ : α → α → Prop := fun v w => ∃ π : Walk G v w, π.IsDirected
    -- Refl: take the trivial walk.
    have hr₀_refl : ∀ v, r₀ v v := fun v => ⟨Walk.nil v, by simp⟩
    -- Trans: concat directed walks.
    have hr₀_trans : ∀ x y z, r₀ x y → r₀ y z → r₀ x z := by
      rintro x y z ⟨π₁, h₁⟩ ⟨π₂, h₂⟩
      exact ⟨π₁.append π₂, directedWalk_append π₁ π₂ h₁ h₂⟩
    -- Antisymm: under acyclicity, any two-way directed walk closes
    -- into a cycle through `x` (which is in `G` via `E_subset` on
    -- the first forward step of `π₁`).
    have hr₀_antisymm : ∀ x y, r₀ x y → r₀ y x → x = y := by
      rintro x y ⟨π₁, h₁⟩ ⟨π₂, h₂⟩
      by_contra h_neq
      cases π₁ with
      | nil _ => exact h_neq rfl
      | cons s p =>
        cases s with
        | forward h_e =>
          have hx : x ∈ G :=
            CDMG.mem_iff.mpr (Set.mem_prod.mp (G.E_subset h_e)).1
          have h_cycle_dir : ((Walk.cons (.forward h_e) p).append π₂).IsDirected :=
            directedWalk_append _ _ h₁ h₂
          have h_cycle_pos :
              1 ≤ ((Walk.cons (.forward h_e) p).append π₂).length := by
            rw [Walk.length_append, Walk.length_cons]; omega
          exact hac x hx ⟨_, h_cycle_dir, h_cycle_pos⟩
        | backward _ => simp at h₁
        | bidir _ => simp at h₁
    -- Register `r₀` as a partial order; apply Mathlib's Szpilrajn.
    haveI : IsPartialOrder α r₀ :=
      { refl := hr₀_refl, trans := hr₀_trans, antisymm := hr₀_antisymm }
    obtain ⟨s, hs_lin, hrs⟩ := extend_partialOrder r₀
    haveI : IsLinearOrder α s := hs_lin
    -- The strict part of `s` is the topological order.
    refine ⟨fun v w => s v w ∧ v ≠ w, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
    · -- irrefl
      intro v _ ⟨_, hne⟩; exact hne rfl
    · -- trans
      intro v _ w hw u _ ⟨hsvw, hne_vw⟩ ⟨hswu, _⟩
      refine ⟨_root_.trans hsvw hswu, ?_⟩
      intro h_eq
      subst h_eq
      exact hne_vw (_root_.antisymm hsvw hswu)
    · -- trichotomous
      intro v _ w _
      rcases eq_or_ne v w with rfl | hne
      · exact Or.inr (Or.inl rfl)
      · rcases total_of s v w with h | h
        · exact Or.inl ⟨h, hne⟩
        · exact Or.inr (Or.inr ⟨h, hne.symm⟩)
    · -- parent_lt
      intro v w h_pa
      obtain ⟨hv, h_edge⟩ := h_pa
      have hr0 : r₀ v w :=
        ⟨Walk.cons (.forward h_edge) (Walk.nil w), by simp⟩
      have hsvw : s v w := hrs v w hr0
      have hne : v ≠ w := by
        intro h_eq; subst h_eq
        exact hac v hv
          ⟨Walk.cons (.forward h_edge) (Walk.nil v), by simp, by simp⟩
      exact ⟨hsvw, hne⟩
  · -- (⇐) has topological order ⇒ acyclic.
    -- A non-trivial directed walk `v = v_0 → ⋯ → v_n = v` would
    -- force `v_0 r v_1 r ⋯ r v_n = v_0` via `parent_lt` chained by
    -- `trans`, contradicting `irrefl` at `v_0`. No finiteness used.
    rintro ⟨r, hr⟩ v hv ⟨π, h_dir, h_pos⟩
    obtain ⟨_, _, h_rvv⟩ :=
      topo_lt_of_directed_walk_pos hr π h_dir h_pos
    exact hr.irrefl v hv h_rvv
lean_code_without_comments:
theorem isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder
    [Finite α] (G : CDMG α) :
    G.IsAcyclic ↔ G.HasTopologicalOrder := by
  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
  ·

    intro hac

    let r₀ : α → α → Prop := fun v w => ∃ π : Walk G v w, π.IsDirected

    have hr₀_refl : ∀ v, r₀ v v := fun v => ⟨Walk.nil v, by simp⟩

    have hr₀_trans : ∀ x y z, r₀ x y → r₀ y z → r₀ x z := by
      rintro x y z ⟨π₁, h₁⟩ ⟨π₂, h₂⟩
      exact ⟨π₁.append π₂, directedWalk_append π₁ π₂ h₁ h₂⟩

    have hr₀_antisymm : ∀ x y, r₀ x y → r₀ y x → x = y := by
      rintro x y ⟨π₁, h₁⟩ ⟨π₂, h₂⟩
      by_contra h_neq
      cases π₁ with
      | nil _ => exact h_neq rfl
      | cons s p =>
        cases s with
        | forward h_e =>
          have hx : x ∈ G :=
            CDMG.mem_iff.mpr (Set.mem_prod.mp (G.E_subset h_e)).1
          have h_cycle_dir : ((Walk.cons (.forward h_e) p).append π₂).IsDirected :=
            directedWalk_append _ _ h₁ h₂
          have h_cycle_pos :
              1 ≤ ((Walk.cons (.forward h_e) p).append π₂).length := by
            rw [Walk.length_append, Walk.length_cons]; omega
          exact hac x hx ⟨_, h_cycle_dir, h_cycle_pos⟩
        | backward _ => simp at h₁
        | bidir _ => simp at h₁

    haveI : IsPartialOrder α r₀ :=
      { refl := hr₀_refl, trans := hr₀_trans, antisymm := hr₀_antisymm }
    obtain ⟨s, hs_lin, hrs⟩ := extend_partialOrder r₀
    haveI : IsLinearOrder α s := hs_lin

    refine ⟨fun v w => s v w ∧ v ≠ w, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
    ·
      intro v _ ⟨_, hne⟩; exact hne rfl
    ·
      intro v _ w hw u _ ⟨hsvw, hne_vw⟩ ⟨hswu, _⟩
      refine ⟨_root_.trans hsvw hswu, ?_⟩
      intro h_eq
      subst h_eq
      exact hne_vw (_root_.antisymm hsvw hswu)
    ·
      intro v _ w _
      rcases eq_or_ne v w with rfl | hne
      · exact Or.inr (Or.inl rfl)
      · rcases total_of s v w with h | h
        · exact Or.inl ⟨h, hne⟩
        · exact Or.inr (Or.inr ⟨h, hne.symm⟩)
    ·
      intro v w h_pa
      obtain ⟨hv, h_edge⟩ := h_pa
      have hr0 : r₀ v w :=
        ⟨Walk.cons (.forward h_edge) (Walk.nil w), by simp⟩
      have hsvw : s v w := hrs v w hr0
      have hne : v ≠ w := by
        intro h_eq; subst h_eq
        exact hac v hv
          ⟨Walk.cons (.forward h_edge) (Walk.nil v), by simp, by simp⟩
      exact ⟨hsvw, hne⟩
  ·

    rintro ⟨r, hr⟩ v hv ⟨π, h_dir, h_pos⟩
    obtain ⟨_, _, h_rvv⟩ :=
      topo_lt_of_directed_walk_pos hr π h_dir h_pos
    exact hr.irrefl v hv h_rvv
lean_explanation:
The theorem fixes a CDMG `G : CDMG α` over an ambient vertex type `α : Type*` — the LN's silent universe of nodes — and concludes the equivalence `G.IsAcyclic ↔ G.HasTopologicalOrder`. On the left, `IsAcyclic` (def_3_6) reads "no non-trivial directed walk in $G$ closes into a cycle". On the right, `HasTopologicalOrder` (def_3_8) is the existential one-liner `∃ r, IsTopologicalOrder G r` — the LN's prose "$G$ has a topological order".

The single non-data hypothesis is the instance `[Finite α]`, the Prop-valued finiteness typeclass. It codifies the LN's silent assumption "$J \cup V$ is finite", and it is needed only for the ($\Rightarrow$) direction: the LN proof (`graphs.tex` lines 227--245) iteratively extracts a parent-free node from a shrinking induced subgraph, and finiteness is what makes that process terminate. The ($\Leftarrow$) direction never touches the hypothesis.

A few Mathlib idioms a first-time reader may want flagged. `Finite α` is the *propositional* finiteness predicate; its data-valued sibling `Fintype α` additionally packages a concrete enumeration `Finset α`. Because the iff is `Prop`-valued and never mentions an enumeration, `Finite` is the lighter form, and Lean still lets a caller in a `[Fintype α]` context apply the theorem without supplying anything extra — instance synthesis runs `Finite.of_fintype` automatically. When the proof needs an actual enumeration internally, it recovers one via `Fintype.ofFinite`. The instance argument `[Finite α]` is placed between the implicit type `α` and the explicit graph `G`, following Mathlib's binder convention that type-class arguments immediately follow the type they constrain.

The overall shape is the standard "fix a graph, then state an equivalence of two predicates of it" pattern: one biconditional, no further hypotheses beyond the finiteness instance. Downstream callers reach for `.mp` (acyclic $\Rightarrow$ has topological order) or `.mpr` (the reverse direction) to extract whichever half they need.

The proof itself takes a slightly cleaner route than the LN's iterative parent-free pick. The ($\Rightarrow$) direction defines the preorder `r₀ v w := ∃ π : Walk G v w, π.IsDirected` ("reachable by a directed walk"), proves it is reflexive (trivial walk), transitive (walk concatenation), and antisymmetric (a two-way directed walk would close into a cycle, contradicting acyclicity), and then invokes Mathlib's `extend_partialOrder` (Szpilrajn extension) to extend `r₀` to a linear order `s` on `α`. Its strict part `λ v w. s v w ∧ v ≠ w` is the topological order. The ($\Leftarrow$) direction chains `parent_lt` along the steps of a hypothetical directed cycle and triggers `irrefl` at its base.
design_choices:
**Existential right-hand side `G.HasTopologicalOrder`, not the relation-level `IsTopologicalOrder G r`.** The LN's Lem reads "$G$ … has a topological order" — the order is existentially quantified. `HasTopologicalOrder` (defined in `TopologicalOrder.lean`) is exactly the unwrap `∃ r, IsTopologicalOrder G r`, lining up with the LN's "has" verbatim. The alternative iff `G.IsAcyclic ↔ ∀ r, IsTopologicalOrder G r` would be outright false (the trivial relation is never a topological order of a non-empty graph), and the relation-level variant `G.IsAcyclic ↔ IsTopologicalOrder G r` for a *fixed* `r` would be strictly stronger than the LN's statement, which only asserts existence.

**`[Finite α]` instance hypothesis.** def_3_1's `CDMG` is polymorphic in `α` and carries no built-in finiteness, so the LN's silent "$J \cup V$ is finite" must enter as an extra hypothesis somewhere. The LN's own ($\Rightarrow$) proof appeals to it literally — "since $G_i$ is acyclic and finite, it has a node with no parents" — and the iterative parent-free-node construction does not terminate without it. The ($\Leftarrow$) direction does not use the hypothesis, but we still attach it to the iff itself rather than splitting into two lemmas; see below.

**`[Finite α]` (Prop-valued) rather than `[Fintype α]` (data-valued).** The iff is `Prop`-valued and never mentions a concrete enumeration. Mathlib's `linter.unusedFintypeInType` flags `[Fintype α]` in exactly this situation and recommends `[Finite α]` instead. Choosing it here silences the linter, gives the statement the weakest finiteness hypothesis the proof needs, and preserves callability from a `[Fintype α]` context (Lean derives `Finite` from `Fintype` automatically via `Finite.of_fintype`). When the proof phase needs an actual enumeration, it recovers one on the fly via `Fintype.ofFinite α` (from `Mathlib.Data.Fintype.EquivFin`).

**Typeclass `[Finite α]` rather than an explicit `(G.J ∪ G.V).Finite`.** The set-level alternative is strictly weaker — it does not force the ambient carrier to be finite, only the node set — and matches the LN's prose "finiteness of $J \cup V$" more literally. We default to the typeclass form for ergonomics: it carries no explicit argument at use sites, composes directly with the full Mathlib `Finite` / `Fintype` API, and matches the iSCM chapters' default ambient-type assumption. Callers in a more general regime where `α` is uncountable but the chosen CDMG has a finite node set can downshift locally — `Finite α → (G.J ∪ G.V).Finite` is one `Set.toFinite` call away.

**Single bundled iff, not two separate lemmas.** Splitting into `isAcyclic_of_hasTopologicalOrder` and `hasTopologicalOrder_of_isAcyclic` was considered, but the LN states the equivalence as one Lem; bundling matches that prose and lets `simp` / `rw` rewrite freely between the two predicates. Callers who only need one direction reach for `.mp` or `.mpr`.

**Name `isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder`, with `α` implicit, `[Finite α]` between, and `G` explicit.** Dot-projection is intended: downstream callers write `G.isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder.mp ha`, reading naturally as the LN's prose "$G$ is acyclic iff $G$ has a topological order". The binder order — implicit type, then instance argument, then explicit data — is Mathlib's convention for "fix an object, state a property of it" theorems, and parallels every other claim-of-CDMG theorem in this section.
